items.title: SSH keys directory created without mode 0700 — key filenames world-listable on multi-user systems
items.intent: The SSH keys directory must not be listable by other users — mkdirSync must set mode 0700 so the directory contents are not exposed even though individual key files are mode 0600.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts line 37 calls mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) without a mode option. This creates the keys directory (deployDir/keys/) with default permissions (0755 on most Linux systems), making the directory world-readable. While individual key files are mode 0600 (correct), any user on the system can ls the keys directory to enumerate which instance IDs have keys stored. The fix is to pass { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 } to mkdirSync, matching the same protection principle used for credentials.ts. The same issue exists in registry.ts line 57 for the registry parent directory, but that directory only contains the registry file which is already 0600.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/ssh-keygen.ts lines 34-50 — confirm mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true }) has no mode option
Change mkdirSync call to mkdirSync(keysDir, { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 }) — this ensures the directory is created owner-only even if an existing parent was 0755
Read src/cloud/provisioning/registry.ts writeInstanceRegistry() — confirm mkdirSync there also lacks a mode; add mode: 0o700 to that call as well
Run ssh-keygen.test.ts and registry.test.ts — confirm both pass and no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: The keys directory (deployDir/keys/) is created with mode 0700 — stat confirms no group/other read bits
The registry parent directory is created with mode 0700
Existing key file mode 0600 behavior is unchanged
items.groomingNotes: READY. ssh-keygen.ts:37 confirmed missing mode on mkdirSync. Two-file fix: ssh-keygen.ts (primary) + registry.ts (secondary, same pattern). No new deps. Scope: 2 files, 2 lines changed.

items.title: Cloud credentials directory created without mode 0700 — API tokens world-listable on multi-user systems
items.intent: The cloud credentials directory must not be listable by other users — it stores API tokens and must be created with mode 0700 so directory contents are not exposed, matching the protection applied to credentials.ts file itself.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/credentials.ts line 53 calls mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true }) without a mode option when creating the cloud/ directory. This creates the directory with default permissions (0755), making it world-readable. While credentials.json is written with mode 0600 (correct), any user on the system can ls the cloud/ directory to enumerate that cloud credentials exist. Same pattern: registry.ts line 57 and snapshot-registry.ts line 51 both call mkdirSync without mode. registry.ts is already covered by BUG-077. snapshot-registry.ts stores non-sensitive data (provider, region, version, timestamp) but should be consistent. Fix: pass { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 } to all three mkdirSync calls.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/credentials.ts lines 50-55 — confirm mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true }) has no mode option
Change mkdirSync in credentials.ts to mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 })
Read src/cloud/provisioning/snapshot-registry.ts lines 48-53 — confirm mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true }) has no mode option
Change mkdirSync in snapshot-registry.ts to mkdirSync(dir, { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 })
Run credentials tests and snapshot-registry tests — confirm both pass with no regressions
acceptanceCriteria: The cloud/ directory (deployDir/cloud/) is created with mode 0700 — stat confirms no group/other read bits
snapshot-registry parent directory is created with mode 0700
File permissions on credentials.json and snapshots.json remain unchanged at 0600
items.groomingNotes: READY. Three-file fix, same pattern as BUG-077. Primary: credentials.ts:53 (stores API tokens — highest severity). Secondary: snapshot-registry.ts:51 (non-sensitive data, but consistent with security model). registry.ts:57 already covered by BUG-077 — do NOT double-fix. Steps: (1) Read credentials.ts:50-55, change mkdirSync to { recursive: true, mode: 0o700 }. (2) Read snapshot-registry.ts:48-53, same change. (3) Run credentials tests and snapshot-registry tests to confirm no regressions. AC: stat on deployDir/cloud/ shows drwx------ (0700), file permissions unchanged at 0600.

items.title: SSH private key orphaned on disk when firewall creation fails during provisioning
items.intent: When provisioning fails at the firewall step, the SSH private key written to disk must be deleted — no private key file should survive a failed provision regardless of which step failed.
items.description: In src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts, generateSshKeypair() is called at line 121 (before any API calls). If firewall creation fails at line 195-224, the engine destroys the VM and returns early at line 220-224. At this point, the instance has never been added to the registry (addInstance at line 229 has not run), so the keypair at deployDir/keys/<instanceId>.pem is orphaned — it can never be cleaned up by destroyInstance() because the registry has no record of it. Fix: on any early return after generateSshKeypair() succeeds (firewall fail, and the no-IP path at line 169-188 when IP is pending), delete the private key file with rmSync(keypair.privateKeyPath, { force: true }) before returning the error result. Alternatively, always add the instance to registry immediately after keypair generation, before the API calls.
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts lines 119-230 — confirm generateSshKeypair() is called before firewall, and that firewall failure returns without deleting the key
Identify all early-return paths after keypair generation: (a) firewall fail at ~line 220-224, (b) no-IP path at ~line 169-188 (key is registered there, skip cleanup)
In the firewall failure path (~line 207-224), add rmSync(keypair.privateKeyPath, { force: true }) before both return statements that exit after VM destroy
Run provisioning engine tests to confirm no regressions — check that the key file is absent after a simulated firewall failure
acceptanceCriteria: After a simulated firewall creation failure, the SSH private key file at deployDir/keys/<instanceId>.pem does not exist on disk
Successful provisioning still produces the key file and records sshKeyPath in the registry
The no-IP path (instance added to registry with sshKeyPath) is unchanged — key is not deleted in that path
items.groomingNotes: READY. Single-file fix (engine.ts). Firewall fail path at ~line 207-224: VM is destroyed, returns early, but keypair.privateKeyPath file written at line 121 is never cleaned up. No registry entry exists at this point so destroyInstance() can never find it. Fix: rmSync(keypair.privateKeyPath, { force: true }) before both early returns in the firewall failure block. Note: the no-IP path (lines 169-188) DOES add the instance to registry with sshKeyPath — do not add cleanup there; destroyInstance() handles it.

items.title: SSH update uses StrictHostKeyChecking=no — remote identity not verified, MITM possible
items.intent: clawhq deploy update must verify the remote host's identity before executing commands — StrictHostKeyChecking=no accepts any host key, making the update channel vulnerable to MITM on the network path.
items.description: src/cloud/provisioning/update.ts line 143 passes -o StrictHostKeyChecking=no and -o UserKnownHostsFile=/dev/null to ssh. This disables host key verification entirely — any host that responds on instance.ipAddress will be trusted. Since ClawHQ generates the SSH keypair and injects the public key via cloud-init, the host public key is also available at provision time via the provider API (DigitalOcean, Hetzner, AWS, GCP all surface console output or host keys). Fix: during provisioning, record the SSH host public key fingerprint in the instance registry (sshHostKey field on ProvisionedInstance). In executeSsh(), write a temporary known_hosts file with that fingerprint and pass -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes -o UserKnownHostsFile=<tmpfile> instead. If fingerprint is unavailable in registry, fall back to accept-new (not no) and log a warning. Scope: engine.ts (capture host key at provision time), types.ts (sshHostKey field on ProvisionedInstance), update.ts (use known_hosts file).
steps: Read src/cloud/provisioning/types.ts — add optional sshHostKey?: string field to ProvisionedInstance interface

Read src/cloud/provisioning/engine.ts — after createVm() returns an IP, query the provider adapter for the SSH host key fingerprint using adapter.getVmStatus() or a new method; if available, pass it to addInstance()
Read src/cloud/provisioning/registry.ts addInstance() — include sshHostKey in the instance record if provided

Read src/cloud/provisioning/update.ts executeSsh() — replace StrictHostKeyChecking=no with: if sshHostKey is present in instance record, write a temp known_hosts file and pass -o StrictHostKeyChecking=yes -o UserKnownHostsFile=<tmpfile>; if absent, use -o StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new and log a warning
Delete the temp known_hosts file after SSH command completes (success or failure)
Run update tests — confirm StrictHostKeyChecking=no is no longer passed in any SSH invocation
acceptanceCriteria: When an instance has sshHostKey in its registry record, executeSsh() uses StrictHostKeyChecking=yes with a temp known_hosts file
When sshHostKey is absent, executeSsh() uses StrictHostKeyChecking=accept-new (not no) — first-connect behavior, not blind trust
StrictHostKeyChecking=no does not appear in any SSH invocation in update.ts
items.groomingNotes: RE-GROOMED 2026-03-23 (PO review). Priority bumped from should → must. Rationale: ClawHQ has a non-negotiable security principle — 'hardening is automatic, not opt-in'. The update channel using StrictHostKeyChecking=no accepts any host presenting on the instance IP, directly contradicting this principle and the product's positioning as sovereign infrastructure. Minimum viable fix (accept-new instead of no) is a single-line change in update.ts and is sufficient for must-grade AC: StrictHostKeyChecking=no must not appear in any SSH invocation. The full sshHostKey fingerprint capture from provider is a stretch goal — do not block the sprint on it. AC: (1) StrictHostKeyChecking=no replaced with accept-new in update.ts, (2) no regression in update tests. If provider fingerprint capture is feasible, ship it — but accept-new alone satisfies AC.
